claims.claim: As battery capacity increases in LFP pouch cells under thermal abuse, the onset of thermal runaway is postponed, although the severity of the event becomes more pronounced.
claims.quote: our results indicate that, as the battery capacity increases, the onset of thermal runaway is postponed, although the severity of the event becomes more pronounced.

claims.claim: The 67 Ah LFP pouch cell emits approximately 21.60 ± 5e-3 L of gas during thermal runaway, markedly higher than the 6.22 ± 5e-3 L released by the 6 Ah cell.
claims.quote: The 67 Ah cell emits approximately 21.60 ± 5e-3 L of gas, markedly higher than the 6.22 ± 5e-3 L released by the 6 Ah cell.

claims.claim: Methane is identified as the first detectable gas during thermal runaway of LFP pouch cells, and TDLAS and NDIR sensors detect target gases 300 to 700 s before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Methane is identified as the first detectable gas, TDLAS and non-dispersive infrared (NDIR) sensors exhibit superior response-ability, detecting the target gases 300 to 700 s before thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The gas production sequence for LFP pouch cells under thermal abuse follows CH4 first, then HF, then H2, with CO2 and CO detected last.
claims.quote: the sequence of gas detection with the commercial sensor module is CH4, followed by HF and H2, with CO2 and CO being detected last.

claims.claim: TDLAS cannot detect gas after thermal runaway because substantial smoke generated during thermal runaway obstructs the laser path and weakens light intensity to zero.
claims.quote: TDLAS can monitor CH4 generation in the early stage of TR of pouch batteries and detect weak gas leakage before the pouch ruptures. However, it is not suitable for detecting gas after TR. This is due to the substantial smoke generated during TR, which obstructs the laser path and weakens the light intensity.

claims.claim: The gas-to-capacity proportion for LFP pouch cells is approximately 0.54 L/Ah based on a linear fit to experimental data.
claims.quote: The resulting gas-to-capacity proportion, 𝐾𝑔𝑎𝑠 = 0.54 L/Ah, indicates that LFP pouch cells generate less gas compared to NCM cells, which have an average gas-to-capacity proportion closer to 0.4 L/Ah.

claims.claim: The 67 Ah LFP battery thermal runaway occurs 829 s later than the 6 Ah cell under 300 W heating, demonstrating that battery size influences the speed of thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: Battery size influences the speed of TR, the 67 Ah battery has a more intense and rapid rise, with TR occurring 829 s later than the 6 Ah cell.

claims.claim: CH4 evolution from LFP battery anodes is primarily due to the reaction between the anode and electrolyte solvent, especially decomposition of dimethyl carbonate, when battery temperature exceeds 120°C.
claims.quote: When the battery temperature exceeds 120 ◦C, the evolution of CH4 is primarily due to the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte solvent, especially the decomposition of dimethyl carbonate (DMC).

claims.claim: H2 generation in LFP batteries is caused by the reaction between the binder and lithium in the battery anode when temperature exceeds 250°C, with PVdF and CMC reacting with lithium ions to produce H2.
claims.quote: H2 is generated by the reaction between the binder and lithium in the battery anode, which occurs when the temperature exceeds 250 ◦C. At this stage, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) react with lithium ions to produce H2.

claims.claim: In the 67 Ah LFP cell test, the TDLAS system detected CH4 concentration rising to 3.17 ppm before 930 s, approximately 300 s before the thermal runaway event, and the 67 Ah battery began generating significant smoke earlier than the 6 Ah cell.
claims.quote: The earliest response time of the TDLAS system is observed at 930 s. Comparing the temperature curves, it is evident that the 67 Ah battery begins generating significant smoke earlier, approximately 300 s before the TR event occurred.
